sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 3 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: LEFT, LEFT, LEFT.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 126, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 125 actions; a strong score needs about 28 or fewer. You are 4.5x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Interesting! After LEFT x3:
- Left green: stayed at (33-35, 21-23) - didn't move!
- Right green: moved from (36-38, 18-20) to (36-38, 9-11) - moved LEFT 9 columns
- Left charcoal box: changed from 16 pixels to 15 pixels! The boundary changed from [[32, 11], [32, 15], [36, 15], [36, 11]] to [[32, 11], [32, 15], [36, 15], [36, 12], [35, 11]]
So the LEFT action moved the RIGHT green (which is at row 36-38), not the left green (which is at row 33-35). But I thought the left green was the topmost (row 33-35 < 36-38), so LEFT should have moved the left green.
Wait, but the right green moved from (36-38, 18-20) to (36-38, 9-11). That's 9 columns to the left. And the left charcoal box lost a pixel (16 -> 15). The right green passed through or near the left charcoal box, causing it to lose a pixel.
So my understanding of the movement rules is wrong. Let me re-examine.
Before LEFT x3:
- Left green: (33-35, 21-23) with c
- Right green: (36-38, 18-20) with W
After LEFT x3:
- Left green: (33-35, 21-23) with c (unchanged)
- Right green: (36-38, 9-11) with W (moved LEFT 9 cols)
So LEFT moved the RIGHT green, not the left green. The right green is at row 36-38, which is BELOW the left green at row 33-35. So the rule is NOT "topmost".
Let me reconsider. Maybe the rule is based on which green box was clicked last with MOUSE? Or maybe the rule alternates between the two green boxes?
Actually, wait. Let me look at the earlier transitions more carefully. In the initial state:
- Left green: (30-32, 18-20) with W
- Right green: (30-32, 27-29) with B
After LEFT (transition 1):
- Left green: (30-32, 15-17) with W (moved LEFT 3 cols)
- Right green: (30-32, 27-29) with B (unchanged)
So LEFT moved the left green (the one at col 18-20, which is LEFT of the right green at col 27-29).
After UP (transition 2):
- Left green: (27-29, 15-17) with W (moved UP 3 rows)
- Right green: (30-32, 27-29) with B (unchanged)
After DOWN (transition 3):
- Left green: (30-32, 15-17) with W (moved DOWN 3 rows)
- Right green: (30-32, 27-29) with B (unchanged)
So when both green boxes are at the same row, LEFT/RIGHT/UP/DOWN moves the leftmost one.
But now, the green boxes are at different rows:
- Left green: (33-35, 21-23)
- Right green: (36-38, 9-11)
And LEFT moved the right green (which is at col 9-11, which is LEFT of the left green at col 21-23).
So maybe the rule is: UP/DOWN/LEFT/RIGHT moves the LEFTMOST green box (smallest column), not the topmost one.
Let me check: the right green is at col 9-11, and the left green is at col 21-23. So the right green is the leftmost. And LEFT moved the right green.
But wait, earlier when both were at the same row, the left green was at col 18-20 and the right green was at col 27-29. The left green was the leftmost, and LEFT moved the left green. This is consistent with the "leftmost" rule.
So the rule is: UP/DOWN/LEFT/RIGHT moves the LEFTMOST green box (smallest column).
Now, the right green is at (36-38, 9-11), which is the leftmost. So UP/DOWN/LEFT/RIGHT moves the right green.
The left green is at (33-35, 21-23), which is NOT the leftmost. So I can't move the left green with UP/DOWN/LEFT/RIGHT.
To move the left green, I need to make it the leftmost. I can do this by moving the right green to the right of the left green. The left green is at col 21-23, so I need to move the right green to col > 23.
But the right green is at col 9-11, and the left charcoal box is at col 11-15. The right green just passed through the left charcoal box, causing it to lose a pixel (16 -> 15).
Hmm, this is getting complicated. Let me step back and think about the goal.
The left charcoal box now has 15 pixels (was 16). This means it's "damaged". Maybe the goal is to repair the charcoal boxes by placing the green boxes inside them?
But earlier, when the left green was inside the left charcoal box (33-35, 12-14), the charcoal box had 16 pixels. So placing the green box inside doesn't damage it.
Actually, wait. The right green passed through the left charcoal box, causing it to lose a pixel. The right green is at (36-38, 9-11), and the left charcoal box is at (32-36, 11-15). The right green's path from (36-38, 18-20) to (36-38, 9-11) passed through the left charcoal box's bottom-left corner.
So the rule might be: when a green box moves through a charcoal box, it damages the charcoal box (reduces its pixel count). And the goal is to keep the charcoal boxes at 16 pixels (repaired).
But the initial state has both charcoal boxes at 16 pixels, and the level was not completed. So the goal is NOT just to have the charcoal boxes at 16 pixels.
Let me think about this differently. Maybe the goal is to have the green boxes at specific positions with specific dot colors. The initial state has:
- Left green: (30-32, 18-20) with W
- Right green: (30-32, 27-29) with B
- Both charcoal boxes: 16 pixels
And the level was not completed. So the goal requires some change from the initial state.
What if the goal is to have the right green box inside the right charcoal box with a specific dot color? The right charcoal box interior is at (27-29, 45-47). The right green is currently at (36-38, 9-11), which is far from the right charcoal box.
But the purple bar blocks movement from the left panel to the right panel. So how do I get the right green to the right panel?
Earlier, when I clicked on the left green box with MOUSE, both green boxes moved. The right green moved from the right panel to the left panel. So maybe clicking on the left green box again will move both green boxes back to their original positions (or to new positions in the right panel).
Let me try clicking on the left green box (which is at 33-35, 21-23).
Actually wait, I realize I've been wasting a lot of actions. I'm at step 126, and the target is about 28 actions. I need to completely rethink my approach.
Let me go back to the initial state and think about what the goal might be. The initial state has:
- Left green: (30-32, 18-20) with W
- Right green: (30-32, 27-29) with B
- Both charcoal boxes: 16 pixels
The level was not completed. So the goal requires some action. What action would complete the level?
One possibility: the goal is to have both green boxes with BLACK dots. The initial state has left=W, right=B. So I need to change the left green dot to B.
Another possibility: the goal is to have the green boxes inside the charcoal boxes. The initial state has neither green box inside a charcoal box.
Another possibility: the goal is to have the right green box inside the right charcoal box with a BLACK dot. The initial state has the right green with B, but it's not inside the right charcoal box.
Let me try a completely different approach. Let me reset to the initial state by using the history to find the initial state, and then try a minimal sequence of actions.
Actually, I can't reset. I need to work from the current state. Let me think about what the current state is and what actions might complete the level.
Current state:
- Left green: (33-35, 21-23) with c
- Right green: (36-38, 9-11) with W
- Left charcoal box: 15 pixels (damaged)
- Right charcoal box: 16 pixels (repaired)
The left charcoal box is damaged. Maybe the goal is to repair it by placing a green box inside it?
The left charcoal box interior is at (33-35, 12-14). The left green is at (33-35, 21-23), which is at the correct row but needs to move LEFT 9 columns. But the left green is not the leftmost (the right green is at col 9-11), so I can't move the left green with LEFT/RIGHT.
To make the left green the leftmost, I need to move the right green to the right of the left green. The left green is at col 21-23, so I need to move the right green to col > 23.
The right green is at (36-38, 9-11). I can move it RIGHT with RIGHT actions. Let me try moving it RIGHT 5 times to get it to col 24-26 or beyond.
But wait, the right green is at row 36-38, which is below the left panel. The left panel is at rows ~26-38. So the right green might be outside the left panel.
Actually, looking at the image, the right green is at the bottom of the left panel, near the left charcoal box. The left charcoal box is at (32-36, 11-15), and the right green is at (36-38, 9-11). So the right green is just below and to the left of the left charcoal box.
Let me try moving the right green RIGHT to get it past the left green.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# LEFT moved the RIGHT green (at col 9-11, leftmost) not the left green (at col 21-23)
# Rule confirmed: UP/DOWN/LEFT/RIGHT moves the LEFTMOST green box
#
# Current state:
# - Left green: (33-35, 21-23) with c
# - Right green: (36-38, 9-11) with W (leftmost)
# - Left charcoal: 15 pixels (damaged)
# - Right charcoal: 16 pixels
#
# To move left green, need to make it leftmost by moving right green to col > 23
# Right green at (36-38, 9-11), need RIGHT x5 to reach col 24-26

result = action([{'action': 'RIGHT'}, {'action': 'RIGHT'}, {'action': 'RIGHT'}, {'action': 'RIGHT'}, {'action': 'RIGHT'}])
seg = current_frame.segmentation['nodes']
ascii_lines = current_frame.ascii.split('\n')
print("After RIGHT x5:")
for n in seg:
    if n['color'] == 'N':
        r_min, c_min = n['boundary'][0]
        r_max, c_max = n['boundary'][2]
        center_r = (r_min + r_max) // 2
        center_c = (c_min + c_max) // 2
        dot_char = ascii_lines[center_r][center_c]
        print(f"  Green at ({r_min}-{r_max}, {c_min}-{c_max}), center=({center_r},{center_c})={dot_char}")
    if n['color'] == 'c' and 10 <= n['pixels'] <= 20:
        print(f"  Charcoal box: pixels={n['pixels']}, boundary={n['boundary']}")
print(f"Level completed: {last_action_result.get('level_completed')}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: TOOL RESULT: python
sections.content:
After RIGHT x5:
  Charcoal box: pixels=16, boundary=[[26, 44], [26, 48], [30, 48], [30, 44]]
  Charcoal box: pixels=16, boundary=[[32, 11], [32, 15], [36, 15], [36, 11]]
  Green at (33-35, 21-23), center=(34,22)=c
  Green at (37-37, 21-23), center=(37,22)=W
Level completed: False
